data.title: Apple: Tim Cook Exits At The Top
data.description:
Apple Inc. (AAPL) heads into the seasonally slow period with Tim Cook set to exit the CEO position now. The tech giant is not in a particularly strong operational position with constant failures on implementing AI. My investment thesis remains ultra Bearish on the stock as the iPhone replacement cycle ends and the future sales catalyst is unknown.

Pent Up Demand

Apple is set to report FQ2'26 earnings on Thursday, April 30, with the consensus analyst estimates as follows:

  • EPS of $1.94 per share, up 17.9% YoY
  • Revenue of $109.73 billion, up 15.1% YoY

The tech giant is set to follow up the solid FQ1 report with another strong quarter. Apple had gone 4 years without quarterly revenue growth topping 10%.

Apple reported a surprise FQ1 revenue beat topping $5 billion due to apparent pent-up-demand for the iPhone. The company reported iPhone revenue surge 23% YoY to $85.3 billion, coming off a 4-year period where sales growth regularly were near the flatline.

The company has already apparently lost the foldable iPhone as a catalyst. DigiTimes suggests the foldable phone has been delayed by a couple of months to at least August, but other reports have the iPhone Fold slipping into 2027.

The product is slated to cost $2,000 to $2,500 and likely will face initial product shortages. Apple struggled selling the Apple Vision Pro with the product having a $3,500 starting price, questioning how many consumers want a product costing double the typical iPhone.

The pent-up-demand cycle fits perfectly with IDC forecasts for iPhone sales to slip this year. As highlighted in prior research, Apple increasing faces issues with smart glasses from Meta Platforms (META) and other vendors.

The memory chip shortage should contribute to a far tougher market ahead with IDC now predicting a dramatic 13% decline in global smartphone sales. The forecast is for just a slight revenue decline due to better results for the large vendors like Apple with only a modest rebound in 2027.

CEO Exit

Tim Cook is set to move to the Executive Chairman position on September 1 after being at the helm since replacing legendary co-founder Steve Jobs on August 24, 2011. John Ternus is taking over the CEO position following his current role as Senior Vice president of hardware engineering.

Mr. Cook has had an exceptional career at Apple, but his biggest success was improving the valuation multiple investors pay for the stock. Apple regularly traded at 10x forward EPS targets and the stock now trades at over 31x FY26 EPS targets.

The stock is up nearly 1,900% since Tim Cook took over while revenues haven't even grown 300%. Mr. Ternus faces a difficult period not so dissimilar to the scenario when Steve Ballmer took over at Microsoft (MSFT) following Bill Gates.

After the tech bubble, Microsoft famously went through a period from 2000 to 2014 where the company grew the business, but the stock was flat to down the whole 14-year period. The market wasn't convinced Mr. Ballmer had the tech giant on the right path and Microsoft saw the valuation shrink his whole tenure.

The ironic part is that both the Tim Cook era at Apple and the Steve Ballmer era at Microsoft lasted close to 15 years with very similar revenue growth during the period of 200% to 300%. The stocks fared far different with Tim Cook generating 1,900% gains while Ballmer saw the stock fall during this period.

The new Apple CEO will enter the position with Apple in a questionable AI strategy. The company has to use Gemini from Google (GOOG) to build a foundational model to fully get Siri and Apple Intelligence off the ground later this year while other tech giants are full-speed ahead building AI services.

Apple has already seen crucial App Store revenue growth slip to only ~5% in FQ1 with the U.S. market essentially flat. If the tech giant was seeing any real benefit, the company would see subscriptions for AI services lead to a boom in growth.

Whether due to pent-up-demand trailing off after FQ2, memory prices impacting iPhone sales, or a delay in the iPhone Fold, Apple is forecast to watch sales slip back to minimal mid-single digit growth rates for the next few years through at least FY28.

Unless Apple changes this scenario, the stock is outrageously priced with a new CEO taking over the helm and ongoing product issues with both AI and the newest hardware product in the iPhone Fold. The stock trades at 31x the FY26 EPS of $8.51 while a more logical valuation for the above growth is only a 15x multiple, or $128.

The Steve Ballmer scenario is on the table for the new Apple CEO. Any lack of execution will leave investors on the sidelines for an extended period while the company could continue growing at the 5% rate predicted by analysts.

Naturally, Apple has a long history now of trading at elevated levels. The stock could rise, as valuation is never a limiting factor. The company could even finally execute on an AI strategy with the new CEO figuring out a way for the company to monetize future AI uses on Apple devices.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Apple has oddly held near all-time highs despite long-time CEO with a surprise exit and the business headed to a difficult few years with slowing iPhone demand and the lack of a monetizable AI product. Tim Cook appears to be exiting just as the company has produced a couple of solid quarters.

Investors should use any pop on a strong FQ2 post-earnings rally as an opportunity to cash out at a premium valuation while risks mount under a new CEO.

data.title: A Bear Market Is Due: I'm Buying This Industry Anyway
data.description:
Lately, I’ve been watching a ton of interviews with “expert” economists and reading the bears on the broader markets (both articles and comments) here on Seeking Alpha.

One pattern keeps showing up. I see a certain level of frustration that the markets are not behaving the way they "should".

I mean, consumer sentiment is at all time lows, gas prices in the U.S. are still over $4/gallon, the CME Group survey is now showing December 2027 as the earliest date for a rate cut, Hormuz is still blocked with the US turning back Iranian tankers, and there are no early signs of a peace deal.

Despite all that, the SPY is barely $5 below the ATHs as I'm writing this piece. The music is still playing, and in my view, despite the near-term uncertainty, it’s likely to get louder as the year goes on.

However, I don't think the tide will lift all the boats equally. In fact, I am hyperselective with the pockets of the market that I've been buying over the last few weeks. More than ever, I think we are in a stock-picker’s market.

In this piece, I discuss my favourite industry: semiconductors. That said, this analysis largely stays at the industry level, and I don't cover the bull case behind the specific stocks that I own in this sector (otherwise, this would be a 10k-word article).

I also discuss some of the risks that are worth considering; some of them keep me up at night.

Before I start, a quick side note for readers new to my investing style.

My Investing Style

My investment style is anything but noble. I follow the rhythm of the market, which sophisticated investors often associate with "following the crowd."

Why not pick through the wreckage (i.e., stocks down 90%+ from their ATHs) in foreign markets or unloved industries (think specialty chemicals), where there is clear mispricing for anyone willing to open a balance sheet or cash flow statement?

Time.

I'm not patient enough to see a position in the red for multiple years until the market recognizes its value. Especially not while a clear bull market is unfolding. The cost of opportunity in locking in my capital in deep value plays for years until I see returns (if at all) is way too high in the current AI supercycle.

So, what tune is the market dancing to right now?

Follow Where Hyperscaler CapEx Is Going

The chart below represents the returns of the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOXX) vs the S&P 500 in the last decade.

Notice the S&P 500 is essentially a flat line in comparison, despite returning 247% in that period.

To understand why, take a look at the chart below representing the CapEx forecast for 2026 (as of February this year) vs the last few years:

To put $646B into perspective, take a look at the chart below representing G7 military spending vs hyperscaler capex:

Ok, fine. Nice figures. How much of that CapEx ends up in the semiconductor industry?

Intuitively, I knew it was "a lot", but I couldn't quantify it until Microsoft's Q1 2026 earnings call, when CFO Amy Hood (finally) made the following remark:

Capital expenditures were $37.5 billion, and this quarter, roughly two thirds of our capex was on short-lived assets, primarily GPUs and CPUs.

There you go: the answer is 66%. To me, that's a good enough rough estimate.

Other analysts have recently estimated that semis (GPUs, racks, memory, storage, etc.) account for about 70% of spending on a 10MW data center.

I cannot overstate how important the chart above is. Therefore, it shouldn’t surprise you that my two favorite industries right now are semis and power infrastructure. Let’s start with the first one.

The Semiconductor Industry Bull Case

Let me start with a "fun fact": global semiconductor sales have already moved into a $1 trillion annualized regime.

The latest hard data from the SIA (Semiconductor Industry Association) is showing global semiconductor sales of $88.8 billion in February 2026, up 7.6% month over month and up 61.8% year over year.

If you annualize the February $88.8 billion monthly sales figure, you get approximately $1T in sales.

Looking at the latest results of the largest semiconductor foundry in the world, TSMC (a topic I discussed in this piece), the company reported (in U.S. dollars) Q1 2026 revenue of $35.90 billion, up 40.6% yoy and 6.4% sequentially. Notably, both net income and diluted EPS increased 58.3% yoy, meaning that earnings growth exceeded revenue growth by 23.2 percentage points.

On the guidance, the company guided a Q2 revenue midpoint of $39.6 billion, which implies a sequential growth of about 10.3%. On gross margins, the Q2 GM midpoint of 66.5% is slightly above Q1’s 66.2%. So, we have higher revenue without margin dilution.

The above is "good enough proof" for me to assume that the peak of the AI supercycle (call it a bubble) is not yet in sight.

And that's just the tip of the iceberg. If I consider other giants in the semi space like Nvidia, the company reported Q4 FY2026 revenue of $68.1 billion, up 20% qoq and 73% yoy. On top of that, at NVIDIA GTC 2026, Jensen Huang said NVIDIA now sees at least $1 trillion in revenue from 2025 through 2027.

By the way, this was an increase from the $500 billion Blackwell/Rubin revenue opportunity through 2026 that Jensen had cited in the last earnings call. So, the new GTC figure represents a 2x increase in the visible opportunity, with the horizon extended by just one year.

On the memory side, it turns out that memory chips are the bottleneck for AI training, inference, and datacenter storage. Last week, SK Hynix said that customer requests for HBM supply over the next three years already “far exceed” production capacity.

For reference, TredForce has recently published its forecast for Q2 this year, and the DRAM and NAND flash prices are still showing a strong increase.

This pricing power is clearly visible in the margin story of these players. Back in March, Micron reported Q1 non-GAAP gross margin rising to 74.9% from 56.8% a quarter ago and 37.9% a year earlier.

On the equipment side, ASML raised earlier this month its 2026 net sales guidance to €36B - €40B with a gross margin expected between 51% - 53%. Other companies like SEMI are now projecting 300mm fab equipment spending to rise 18% to $133B in 2026 and 14% to $151B in 2027.

How about valuations? Well, despite the parabolic chart below, Micron is still trading at 9x forward earnings.

Of course, there are expensive pockets within semiconductors, like for example AMD, which is trading at 50x forward earnings. That said, the premium is mostly due to the optionality to take over market share from Nvidia. In fact, that's why Nvidia is trading at just 26x forward earnings.

I'll move now to the risks, as not everything is rainbows and sunshine in this industry.

Risk To The Semiconductor Industry

So, you're essentially timing the peak of the supercycle, right? (some may ask)

Yes, you got it right. What I’m doing here is basically betting I can get out before the AI bubble pops. In fact, when I see the first cockroaches, I will likely short the same players that I'm riding right now on the way up.

To understand why I believe timing the peak of the bubble is possible, I wrote a piece last year that I highly recommend as a read:

Aside from timing the peak of the AI supercycle, there are other (bigger) risks to consider.

Right now, there is a lot of uncertainty around China regarding semiconductor equipment exports. Specifically, the proposed U.S. MATCH Act could block ASML’s DUV tool sales and servicing in China. Some analysts believe the new rules could lead to a possible EPS reduction of up to 10%.

If that proposal goes through, I think the broader semiconductor equipment industry (think Lam Research, Applied Materials, KLA, Tokyo Electron) will go down. By how much and how long is impossible to say.

However, given the 6-month returns of the constituents seen in the chart below, I wouldn't be surprised to see a double-digit correction in this industry.

For disclosure, I own LRCX and AMAT as of this writing, so this risk is front and center for me.

Of course, there are more general risks, like a slowdown in hyperscaler CapEx spending due to overcapacity. Overall, I think this is what will pop the AI bubble. That said, I will only worry about this risk when hyperscalers use the word "digestion" during the earnings calls.

On the memory side, the likes of Micron, SK Hynix, and Samsung have benefited from the pricing hike in memory chip prices due to supply constraints. So far, analysts are projecting DRAM prices up 125% and NAND prices up 234% in 2026, with meaningful relief not expected until late 2027.

When that relief happens, I expect a sharp selloff in these names, as they will lose their earnings power.

Lastly, outside of the AI demand, the auto, industrial, PC, and smartphone markets are anything but exciting. For example, earlier this year, Onsemi missed Q4 revenue, with revenue down 11% and segment declines up to 17%, due to an inventory glut.

In the auto world, global EV registrations fell 11% in February 2026, with China down 32% yoy.

On the PC and smartphone side, PC and smartphone shipments are expected to decline in 2026 due to memory cost inflation (precisely, the same reason why the memory stocks are up right up).

Lastly, one shouldn't forget the bottlenecks. According to some analysts, nearly 40% of U.S. data center projects from the likes of Microsoft, Oracle, and OpenAI that are expected to open in 2026 are at risk of being delayed by at least three months.

So far, none of these players has announced any delays. Heading into the Q1 2026 earnings print, I think such an announcement could be a big downside risk for semiconductor stocks. Why?

Because it may delay orders, pushing the revenue and earnings estimates to the right.

Conclusion

Yes, we are in a bubble. No, I don't think the burst is in sight.

The reason I think we are so in a bubble is that hyperscaler CapEx is way ahead of the upside that AI brings to their businesses. For example, Amazon's CEO Andy Jassy disclosed for the first time the AWS AI revenue.

For reference, the AWS run rate at the start of the year was about $142B. How much do you think of that was driven by AI services?

Half? 75%? 90%?

Well, far from that. The figure is less than 10%. Specifically, AWS’s AI revenue run rate was over $15 billion in Q1 2026. Yes, that's nearly 260x larger than AWS at the same point. However, I cannot help but wonder if burning $200B in CapEx this year justifies $15B in AI revenue.

Overall, I have little to no doubts that we will see a major correction in the markets. However, betting too early on it makes no sense to me. First, you are losing upside from the bull run. Second, you are not entering your short at a favorable price, which means that, even if the correction happens, you capitalize only a small part of it.

Bottom line: I love semis. I own semis. I’ll dance with semis as long as the market keeps playing the music. That said, at the first sign that the music (i.e., hyperscaler CapEx) is slowing down, I’m out.

data.title: Italy's April Confidence Data Signals Sweeping Effects Of Middle East War
data.description:
By Paolo Pizzoli, Senior Economist, Italy, Greece

The impact of the Middle East war and the stalemate in negotiations keeping the Strait of Hormuz almost closed to navigation is apparently spreading through the Italian economy, affecting both consumers and businesses.

Consumer confidence falls further

In March, consumers were the first to feel the hit of the consequences of war in the Middle East. April data shows that the picture has not improved, despite news of the ceasefire. Consumer confidence dropped further to 90.8, the lowest level since January 2023, dragged down by deteriorating expectations for the economic situation. Consumers are increasingly worried about future unemployment and expect higher inflation down the road. This combination is well reflected in decelerating intentions to purchase both durable goods and houses and in growing intentions to save. The stabilisation in retailers' confidence, meanwhile, suggests possible resilience in non-durable consumption.

Manufacturers also feeling the brunt

In manufacturing, the contained deterioration in the orders component and a low level of inventories resulted in a very slight softening in current and expected production. Both the order and production indicators worsened further for instrumental goods – a warning shot for short-term machinery investment development. Interestingly, businesses signalled increasing intentions to revise their prices upwards in the future. For the time being, the war in the Middle East and its energy disruptions seem to point to a pause in the tentative recovery rather than a new plunge into a manufacturing recession.

Construction also hit, but infrastructural exception persists

The deterioration is more marked in the construction sector, where confidence fell by almost three points (though remained above the January 2026 low), dragged down by deteriorating current and expected orders/construction plans. The survey details show that the bulk of the deterioration rests in the residential building component.

The fourth consecutive gain in confidence in the civil engineering component, meanwhile, likely reflects the approaching deadline (end of June) for part of the infrastructural investments foreseen by the EU-funded national recovery plan. Against a backdrop of cooling demand, builders point to higher prices ahead; the inflationary impact of the energy disruption on costs is now also showing up in the construction sector.

Services no longer immune, with tourism most affected

The last bit of bad news is the broad-based decline in market services confidence. After proving resilient in March, confidence fell more than three points in April, dragged down by the tourism component, with transport and storage, services to businesses and information and communication all firmly in negative territory. The tourism drop is likely due to disruptions in flight routes and jet fuel availability, plus prices driven by the war in the Middle East.

As we've seen in other sectors, price pressures are seemingly hitting service sector businesses, which in April signalled increasing intentions to revise prices up later down the road.

A soft second quarter looks increasingly likely

All in all, April's confidence data shows that the impact of the war in the Middle East and related energy price pressures is now impacting all broad sectors of the Italian economy and that the risk of a broader pass-through of energy price pressures is rising by the month. Growth-wise, the second quarter likely started on a weaker footing. We are tentatively pencilling in a flat GDP reading, with substantial risks of contraction if the deadlock in the Strait of Hormuz persists for another few weeks.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: 2 BDC Funds: 1 Is A Buy, The Other Is A Clear No-Go
data.description:
The private credit complex is not that easy to understand and assess.

This asset class has multiple facets, such as different segments (lower middle market up to upper middle market), strategies (first-lien biased vs. equity biased), structures (external vs. internal BDCs), and so on.

In fact, oftentimes investors who are new to the private credit world mix together private credit with the broadly syndicated loan market, high-yield, and equity CLO structures. Theoretically, all this is a form of above-average risk credit, but the underlying nuances are very different, which, obviously, can lead to different outcomes.

Considering that private credit is, after all, a relatively risky area to play in, flying blind here probably wouldn't be the smartest thing to do. This is especially the case now, when the market has assumed a "seek and destroy" mode, looking for any evidence to penalize private credit vehicles or BDCs.

So, one way to reduce the risk of unintentionally stepping on BDC value traps is to traffic into private credit funds. Here I'm talking about vehicles, which carry exposures to private credit but are not BDCs themselves.

Apart from the diversification angle that such kinds of funds could provide, timing-wise, it could be one of the best moments to lean into the diversified fund structures to capture the private credit factor. In other words, since the BDC space is so discounted, the individual stock selection becomes less important than the sector (factor) selection.

With this being said, let me elaborate on two private credit funds.

One of them is a buy; the other is a clear no-go, in my view.

#1: PBDC - Buy

The Putnam BDC Income ETF (PBDC) is one of the two ETF vehicles out there that track the publicly traded BDC space. The other ETF is the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD), which has a much bigger AUM ($1.56 billion vs. $267 million) than PBDC as well as a longer history.

Yet, as opposed to BIZD, PBDC is more actively managed and thus has stronger prospects for a solid performance.

We can see a rather consistent alpha performance since PBDC's incorporation:

The overall strategy of PBDC is to invest in a broad basket of publicly traded BDCs to generate attractive current income.

For example, currently, PBDC yields ~11.4%, which should move the needle for almost any income-seeking investor. Historically, the average yield has been closer to 10%, which is still decent.

Speaking of portfolio, PBDC has deployed capital across 23 individual BDCs, which is exactly what is required to obtain a broad exposure to the space, limiting excessive concentration in potentially wrong names.

Now, if we take a look at PBDC's Top 10 holdings list, we will notice a huge bias towards the well-capitalized and large-cap BDCs. In fact, most of these BDCs carry deep discounts to NAV, with Trinity Capital (TRIN), Main Street Capital (MAIN), and Hercules Capital, Inc. (HTGC) being the only three exceptions, trading above NAV.

On top of that, none of these Top 10 names have been subject to abnormal non-accruals other than FS KKR Capital (FSK), which is indicative of high-quality bias.

So, for investors who are bullish on private credit and/or want to simply dip their toes in this high-yielding area without cherry-picking their own BDCs, PBDC provides a solid option to do so.

Having said that, let me underscore one very important point about why PBDC is not something that I have included in my portfolio (apart from the fact that I'm a true believer in individual stock picking that is based on fundamental deep dives).

There is significant exposure to externally managed BDCs. From the Top 10 list, the three names that I mentioned above are the only ones that are internally managed. Capital Southwest (CSWC), which is another internally managed BDC, consumes ~3% of PBDC's portfolio. So, 80%+ of PBDC's allocations are connected to externally managed BDCs.

The reason why PBDC is so heavily exposed to externally managed BDCs is simple: the largest and most liquid BDCs are placed there. This is very important for an ETF to avoid unfavorable bid/ask spreads, where smaller cap BDCs (with their shallow trading volumes) introduce just too high risks.

All this has nothing to do with the underlying quality of BDCs or somehow enhanced risk-adjusted capital allocation processes.

Now, many investors who have decided to avoid PBDC cite excessive management fees as one of the main reasons for staying away here. On the surface, the fee ratio is very high - ~13.5%. However, the bulk of this is driven by the leverage costs that are associated with the underlying BDC holdings (i.e., borrowing costs paid by the BDC themselves, not the ETF). The pure net expense ratio is 0.75%, which is fully acceptable.

All in all, PBDC is a buy for investors who want access to deeply discounted BDCs but skip the individual stock-picking part.

#2: FSCO - Avoid

FS Credit Opportunities Corp. (FSCO) is a closed-end fund (CEF) through which investors could consider trafficking into the private credit space without cherry-picking individual BDCs.

As opposed to PBDC, FSCO does offer an even broader participation in the "above-average risk" credit complex. For example, it invests in both public and private credit securities, which, among other things, include direct lending (this is where BDCs play), broadly syndicated loans, and high-yield market.

The difference, however, is that FSCO has not distributed its capital across, say, 20+ BDCs, which is the case for PBDC, rendering it, effectively, a fund-of-funds structure.

Instead, FSCO acts more like a separate BDC, but with a broader investment universe. So, I would say that this particular feature renders the "diversification feature" less attractive for FSCO. Simply put, for FSCO we could draw a very close parallel to an individual BDC like, say, Ares Capital (ARCC), which also invests across different verticals (but in the underlying direct lending space).

Now, with that being said, let's take a closer look at the fundamentals.

If we start with the chart, then the situation does not look that promising - i.e., FSCO has significantly underperformed the broader BDC market over the past 12-month period:

There have been several reasons behind FSCO's underperformance, but the main one is related to a combination of eroding NAV and a very weak net investment income profile.

FSCO has experienced a notable NAV contraction due to unfavorable markdowns and realized losses, which is only logical considering its NII profile.

Namely, almost 22% of FSCO's total income stems from PIK (payment in kind, which is a non-cash item), while the BDC sector average is 8.5%. This means that on a net investment income, or NII, per unit level, the PIK is even more pronounced.

What makes the situation go from bad to worse is the fact that FSCO's current dividend (which was recently cut) is underwater - i.e., the dividend coverage is ~87%.

Furthermore, ~17% of FSCO's portfolio is located outside of the safest loan investment class that can be taken in the private credit industry, which is the senior secured first lien.

It means that a notable part of FSCO's exposure lies in inherently riskier products, which, in conjunction with significant PIK and undercovered dividend, doesn't render the picture enticing.

Now, theoretically, we might argue that all this is already priced into the cake, as the market has assigned a discount of 28%. I agree that the majority of the risks are baked in. However, given that BDC sector median P/NAV stands at 0.76x (i.e., 24% discount), I don't see a notable bargain opportunity here.

I would rather lean into PBDC, which is a) more diversified and b) comprised of higher quality fundamentals. Assuming extra risks in the form of FSCO (which has underfunded dividend) just for a slightly deeper discount is not the smartest idea to implement in the current environment, where private credit as an asset class has fallen under huge scrutiny from investors (e.g., each smallest mistake will most likely lead to sizeable repricing).

data.title: Asset Allocation Outlook Q2 2026: Beyond The Conflict
data.description:
By Joe Amato and Jeff Blazek

Executive Summary

Looking through the significant near-term disruption of the Middle East conflict, the Asset Allocation Committee retains its broadly constructive medium-term outlook on growth and risk assets. In a more complex investment environment, traditional diversifiers have not performed as expected, policy expectations have repriced, and war-driven fiscal and inflation pressures have pushed government bond yields higher. The range of potential outcomes is wide, yet the structural case for selective exposure to risk assets remains intact, with the Committee upgrading segments where valuations and themes are compelling and trimming where the risk-reward has become harder to justify.

Equities Outlook

Valuation Reset

Despite significant market volatility and rising economic risk from the Middle East conflict, the Committee remains broadly constructive on global equities, underpinned by the U.S. and emerging markets. Valuation multiple contraction and strengthening earnings estimates have created compelling entry points in U.S. large caps, but also in small and mid-caps where the broadening-out thesis—while not yet fully assured—remains a credible and increasingly attractively priced opportunity. However, Europe is confronted with starkly different headwinds; acute energy vulnerability, rising fiscal pressures and deteriorating growth prospects make it the most exposed developed equity market. Japan is better positioned given ongoing structural reform momentum. In emerging markets, compelling structural growth themes and attractive valuations across China, India and Brazil support the Committee’s continued positive stance. China and India’s energy import reliance creates near-term sensitivity, but the fundamental investment view in both markets remains sufficiently robust to maintain conviction, reinforcing a broader view that, even in a more complex and demanding environment, the structural case for selective equity exposure remains intact.

Key Positional Changes vs. 1Q 2026

  • The AAC remains broadly constructive on equities, maintaining an overall overweight in global stocks—including in emerging markets and U.S. small and mid-caps—and upgrading U.S. all cap and large caps to overweight from at target.
  • The Committee maintains its at target view on developed market non-U.S. equities, and continues to hold an overweight in Japan, but has downgraded European equities to underweight from at target.
  • In emerging markets, the AAC maintains its overweights in China, India and Brazil.

Fixed Income Outlook

Mispriced Opportunity

The fixed income landscape has been reshaped by the conflict and sharp shifts in central bank policy expectations. As a result, the Committee sees selective opportunity—most compellingly at the front end of the U.S. yield curve, where markets are pricing in tightening that is unlikely to materialize. The Committee believes instead that central banks facing a growth and liquidity shock are far more likely to prolong the cycle than tighten, and the market’s mispricing of that path has created an attractive entry point. It also reinforces a favorable view of high-quality fixed income broadly, where a meaningful valuation reset has restored the appeal of investment grade credit. The constructive view extends to non-U.S. developed markets (see Up for Debate section), where overweights in European and Japanese government and corporate bonds offer attractive risk-adjusted returns as markets recalibrate growth expectations lower. Beyond developed markets, the Committee has adjusted its position in emerging markets debt after a period of relative outperformance. A stronger dollar and energy-driven terms-of-trade deterioration have complicated the near-term outlook for EM FX. The Committee’s longer-term constructive view on EM currencies nonetheless remains in place.

Key Positional Changes vs. 1Q 2026

The Committee has upgraded its views on U.S. government securities to at target from underweight and U.S. investment-grade fixed income to overweight from at target based on more enticing valuations overall.

While the AAC has maintained overweights in non-U.S. developed market and investment-grade corporate bonds, it has downgraded its position on emerging markets debt, including sovereign hard and local currency bonds, to at target from overweight.

In currencies, the Committee has downgraded sterling to underweight from at target and slightly adjusted down its overweight on EM FX.

Real & Alternative Assets Outlook

Tactical Recalibration

The headwinds facing private equity are converging; legacy portfolios concentrated in software and services carry growing AI disruption risk, rising refinancing costs are compressing leveraged returns, and geopolitical uncertainty are challenging the illiquidity premium at current valuations. Yet the picture is not uniformly cautious. The medium-term case for private equity, particularly in secondaries, remains strong, and well-structured new investments are generally more attractive today than they have been in some time. New institutional commitments to private corporate direct lending, by contrast, are already commanding tighter covenants, higher spreads and better return profiles, making these deals more attractive in the near term. Private real estate continues to warrant an overweight— offering income stability, inflation protection, diversification, and capital appreciation driven by AI-related data center demand.

In commodities, the structural case is compelling, but for different reasons. The conflict in the Middle East has triggered a fundamental repricing of energy that is unlikely to reverse: supply constraints are structural rather than cyclical, and some level of risk premium in energy is expected to persist beyond a near-term resolution. Alongside hedged strategies, commodities occupy a rare position in the current portfolio, offering a return opportunity and an effective hedge at a time when traditional diversifiers have struggled to provide either.

Key Positional Changes vs. 1Q 2026

  • The Committee has downgraded private equity to at target from overweight, consistent with its at target view for private debt.
  • Commodities remain an overweight alongside private real estate, with hedged strategies at target.

Up for Debate

EM Equities: Shaken, Not Disturbed

The Committee debated whether to downgrade emerging market equities to neutral or to maintain the overweight established last quarter. The energy import dependence of key EM economies was the central concern, reflected in the equity markets of China, India, Korea and Taiwan being among the hardest hit in the conflict’s opening weeks. Yet these markets have shown resilience, supported by recognition of the longer-term structural AI and technology theme across China, Korea and Taiwan - a multiyear thesis that a near-term energy shock does not derail - and India’s domestic growth dynamics. More broadly, the EM universe is now offering a more compelling entry point, particularly in markets where the structural growth thesis remains intact and the near-term energy shock is a cyclical rather than fundamental headwind. Brazil, as a commodity exporter and energy-independent economy, stands out as a natural beneficiary of the current energy environment. Overall, the Committee’s view is that in a de-escalation scenario, risk markets are likely to snap back to the themes in place earlier this year, and reducing exposure at that moment risks selling into a rebound.

Up for Debate

Non-U.S. DM Fixed Income: From Cuts to Hikes?

The debate over non-U.S. developed market fixed income was one of the meeting’s most substantive, and the Committee resolved to hold its position. The bear case is real. War-driven fiscal spending, rising defense commitments, and energy subsidy costs are building structural pressure on long-end European curves. The risk of an inflation shock, especially if oil remains elevated, is an asymmetric threat that faces Europe far more acutely than the U.S. The ECB adds to the uncertainty. Conflicting signals from within the central bank raise the risk of a policy mistake. Yet the constructive case is well grounded. The belly of the curve—where overweights in German bunds and U.K. gilts are concentrated—offers compelling risk-reward. Japanese government bonds add a further opportunity: with Japan funding itself heavily at the front end and domestic capital rotation supporting demand, JGBs offer a return profile that complements the European overweights. This is also not 2022; rates are not at zero, and the discussion concerns a handful of hikes, not 500 basis points of tightening. A de-escalation scenario would likely reverse much of the recent move. Downgrading risks locking in a position that would quickly need unwinding. The Committee held, but the debate remains open.

Conclusion

Beyond the Conflict

The Middle East conflict has not undermined the investment case for global growth and risk assets, but it has raised the bar for allocators, demanding informed, experienced judgment that distinguishes disciplined conviction from reactive positioning. Diversifiers have fallen short, yield curve dynamics have shifted, and the range of outcomes remains wide. Yet the foundation for selective conviction remains intact: earnings have proven resilient, valuations have reset in pockets of the market, and the rates environment bears little resemblance to the inflationary rupture of 2022.

A reopening of the Strait of Hormuz would be a welcome development, easing one of the most acutely damaging near-term risks to global supply chains and energy flows. Yet a geopolitical risk premium on energy is likely to endure, and the structural damage to energy supply chains will take considerably longer to repair. Nevertheless, the breadth of interests aligned toward de-escalation and a sustainable peace, together with the durability of underlying economic resilience, support a return to the productivity gains and nominal growth that defined the year’s opening months.

The disruption is real, but the Committee does not believe it is a reason to retreat. The bearish case has not been made compellingly enough to justify a dramatic reallocation. Staying the course on long-term strategic asset allocation, through the inevitable twists and turns ahead, remains the defining discipline of this environment.

Key investment implications

Equities: Resist the pull of broad market beta; the most compelling equity opportunities are found where valuations have reset and structural growth themes remain intact, not where indices point.

Fixed Income: With markets mispricing the policy tightening cycle, the front-end and high-quality credit offer the most attractive risk-adjusted returns in fixed income while long-end exposure warrants restraint.

Real and Alternative Assets: In a world where traditional diversifiers have failed and energy markets have been structurally repriced, hedged strategies and commodities now offer what conventional portfolio hedges no longer can.

This material is provided for informational purposes only and nothing herein constitutes investment, legal, accounting or tax advice, or a recommendation to buy, sell or hold a security. The views expressed herein are generally those of Neuberger’s Asset Allocation Committee, which comprises professionals across multiple disciplines, including equity and fixed income strategists and portfolio managers. The Asset Allocation Committee reviews and sets long-term asset allocation models, establishes preferred near-term tactical asset class allocations and, upon request, reviews asset allocations for large, diversified mandates. Tactical asset allocation views are based on a hypothetical reference portfolio. The views and recommendations of the Asset Allocation Committee may not reflect the views of the firm as a whole, and Neuberger advisors and portfolio managers may recommend or take contrary positions to the views and recommendations of the Asset Allocation Committee.
